title: Apple looks to Asian markets to break out of niche role with Xserve (HostingTech)
author: Anne Zieger (HostingTech magazine, correspondent)
abstract: HostingTech magazine (November 2002) by Anne Zieger profiles Apple's campaign to use the Xserve 1U rack-mount server (running Mac OS X Unix-based OS) to break into Asian datacenter markets — its niche markets being media/entertainment, broadband/Internet services, education/research, and biotechnology/life sciences. Asian server market (excluding Japan) Q1 2002: $1.3B, down from $1.4B prior year (-7%) per IDC. Apple Q&A: CEO Steve Jobs; Cupertino CA; founded 1977; ~9,612 employees; 2001 revenues $5.36B; URL apple.com. Quotes from multiple analysts. Tim Bajarin (Creative Strategies): 'Apple is quite committed to going after this server marketplace, and over time could be a worthy competitor.... It is our opinion that the Asian market could be Apple's best place to gain initial ground.' Tim Deal (analyst): 'It's easy to use, and physically easy to maintain. It should appeal to both the veteran I.T. manager and to new I.T. managers.' Daniel Duerr (Grey Zone — Xserve channel partner): 'The cost for disk storage is unprecedented. This is one of the big things Apple did right.' Quote from Peter Kastner, chief research officer with Aberdeen Group: 'For Apple's computer-intensive imaging customers, the Xserve offers a less expensive and [more scalable product] not available from Apple until now, with the ability to port many Unix/Linux applications easily.' Closing Kastner caution: 'No one should expect miracles overnight. Apple has a good product but needs to build channels and acceptance, while worldwide market conditions are poor at best.' Storage cost analysis: Sony 480 GB NAS retails $4,100-$4,400; Maxtor 480 GB ~$3,600; fully-loaded Xserve gives same storage plus more features for similar price.
importance_rationale: Important Aberdeen/Kastner quote on Apple's first sustained server-market push (Xserve, May 2002 launch). Companion to Batch 19 SVBI Tanner article. Captures Kastner's chief-research-officer voice on Apple at a strategic inflection point and demonstrates Aberdeen's coverage of Apple at a time when Apple was not yet a major enterprise vendor.
relevance_rationale: Apple ultimately discontinued Xserve in January 2011 — Kastner's caution about channels and acceptance proved warranted. Modern Apple silicon Macs in datacenters (e.g., AWS EC2 Mac, Apple Vision Pro app build farms) trace lineage to the Xserve effort. The 2002 Asian server market dynamic (IDC reporting -7% decline) bears thematic similarity to subsequent regional market cycles.
prescience_rationale: Kastner's 'no one should expect miracles overnight' caution was vindicated: Apple's enterprise/server ambitions in 2002 did not produce broad market share before Xserve was discontinued in 2011. However, the 'channels and acceptance' frame remained the right diagnostic. Bajarin's 'worthy competitor' optimism was less accurate. Kastner's specific call that Xserve would best serve Apple's 'computer-intensive imaging customers' with Unix/Linux portability was the actual market reality during Xserve's 9-year life.
